company.name: Prologis
company.description: Prologis is an industrial REIT focused on logistics real estate, customer operations services, energy solutions, mobility infrastructure, and related private-capital platforms.

overview.title: Global logistics real estate platform
paragraphs: Prologis owns and manages a large global logistics real estate platform serving thousands of customers across warehouse, fulfillment, and distribution use cases.
The company has increasingly positioned the platform as more than leased square footage, combining real estate with energy, mobility, data infrastructure, and private-capital capabilities.

overview.title: Adjacency into customer infrastructure
paragraphs: Prologis Essentials packages operational services such as racking, forklifts, generators, solar, EV charging, and other site-level infrastructure around the company's logistics facilities.
That adjacency increases customer switching costs because warehouse location, power access, leasing, and operational equipment can become bundled into a single landlord relationship.

moatNarrative: Prologis' moat is built around scarce logistics locations, a very large global portfolio, customer relationships with major shippers and retailers, access to low-cost capital, and the ability to develop or reposition sites near dense consumption markets.

The moat is not software-like absolute control: tenants can move, build-to-suit competition exists, and warehouse demand cycles with trade, interest rates, and supply-chain investment. Still, location scarcity and scale make replacement slow and capital intensive.
decentralizationNarrative: The real estate layer is difficult to decentralize because it depends on land, zoning, grid interconnection, financing, and long-lived physical assets. Open software can improve warehouse operations, but it cannot cheaply recreate urban infill logistics portfolios.

The more promising decentralization pressure sits around the service bundle: open energy management, interoperable EV charging, local solar, cooperative warehouse ownership, and open automation stacks can reduce dependence on one integrated landlord-service provider.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 76-100; market data refreshed on 2026-05-26 using CompaniesMarketCap and StockAnalysis.

moat.rationale: Scarce logistics locations, a 1.3B+ square-foot global platform, more than 6,500 customers, private-capital scale, and energy/mobility adjacencies support a strong but physical-asset-bound moat.

decentralizability.rationale: Land ownership, zoning, financing, and grid access make the core warehouse portfolio hard to decentralize, though customer energy, charging, and automation services are more open to protocol and open-source substitution.

profitability.rationale: Prologis reported durable earnings, strong occupancy, positive same-store NOI growth, and full-year 2025 Core FFO per share of $5.86 excluding net promote expense, indicating strong REIT profitability despite capital intensity.

peRatio.rationale: StockAnalysis listed Prologis' trailing P/E ratio at 36.74 around the May 22, 2026 close; REIT valuation should also be assessed against FFO/AFFO, so this is a conventional equity metric rather than a complete valuation view.

marketCap.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported Prologis' market capitalization at approximately $136.02 billion as of May 2026.

products.name: Logistics real estate
products.summary: Global warehouse, fulfillment, and distribution facilities positioned near major consumption and transportation markets.
products.whyItMatters: Logistics real estate controls where goods are staged, sorted, charged, repaired, and dispatched, so it shapes the physical cost structure of e-commerce, retail distribution, and industrial supply chains.
replacementSketch: A credible replacement would not be a single website or app. It would combine shared ownership of warehouse nodes, transparent local availability data, interoperable automation, and financing models that let smaller operators pool demand.
Open tools can reduce dependency on proprietary site services, but the land and permitting layer means replacement would be gradual and geographically uneven.

alternatives.name: openTCS
alternatives.summary: Open-source transportation control software for automated guided vehicles and other non-continuous conveyors inside industrial facilities.

openness.rationale: The project publishes source code under an open-source license and is maintained by Fraunhofer IML.

decentralizationFit.rationale: openTCS decentralizes a warehouse automation control layer, but it does not replace ownership of the warehouse site or broader leasing platform.

readiness.rationale: The software targets real AGV and conveyor control use cases and is maintained by a logistics research institute, but deployment still requires systems integration and facility-specific engineering.

costLeverage.rationale: Open automation control can reduce vendor lock-in and integration costs for smaller facilities, though hardware, safety certification, and installation remain expensive.

disruptionConcepts.name: Federated warehouse capacity exchange
disruptionConcepts.summary: A federated network of independently owned warehouses, 3PLs, and local cooperatives could publish standardized capacity, equipment, power, and service-level data into interoperable markets rather than forcing shippers into vertically bundled landlord platforms.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept shifts market structure from a few scaled landlords bundling space and services toward many smaller operators coordinating through shared discovery, reputation, and contracting rails.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through federated listing, reputation, and settlement rather than Bitcoin as a required base layer; local operators could retain control of facilities while participating in shared capacity markets.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Warehouses publish machine-readable capacity, dock windows, handling equipment, energy availability, and pricing; shippers or 3PLs route demand through federated brokers that can interoperate across regional networks.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Reputation, signed facility attestations, booking deposits, carrier check-ins, IoT-backed occupancy events, and dispute histories constrain fake listings and nonperformance, while repeated local transactions make reputation costly to abandon.
failureModes: Large shippers may still prefer a single balance-sheet-backed landlord for reliability and indemnity.
Facility data can be spoofed or selectively reported unless occupancy, insurance, safety, and fulfillment records are independently auditable.
Local zoning and capital constraints can prevent enough high-quality nodes from joining in dense urban markets.
adoptionPath: Start with overflow storage, seasonal capacity, and regional 3PL networks where switching costs are lower.
Standardize facility metadata, service-level terms, and dispute resolution before attempting mission-critical fulfillment commitments.
Layer cooperative financing or shared ownership on top of proven high-utilization nodes.

decentralizationFit.rationale: A federated capacity market directly reduces dependence on a single scaled landlord by letting independent sites coordinate supply.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Capacity marketplaces and logistics software are plausible, but trust, liability, quality control, and shipper procurement preferences are significant coordination barriers.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Software standards and open automation can be built now, but matching Prologis' location quality and financing scale requires slow physical-world adoption.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept could pressure overflow and service margins before it threatens core infill assets, because Prologis' prime locations remain scarce.

disruptionConcepts.name: Local microfulfillment and repair cells
disruptionConcepts.summary: Distributed microfactories and local repair cells could move a subset of spare parts, returns processing, light assembly, and customization closer to end users, reducing dependence on large centralized logistics nodes for long-tail inventory.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: If more products can be repaired, refurbished, customized, or fabricated locally, the demand profile for some centralized warehouse space shifts from storing finished goods toward coordinating designs, materials, and local production capacity.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The decentralization role is manufacturing and repair locality: open designs, local operators, and reusable materials reduce the need for a central owner to intermediate every physical flow.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Manufacturers, local shops, and customers coordinate through open design files, inventory recipes, repair records, quality standards, and local fulfillment directories.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Quality is constrained through signed design versions, serial-number repair logs, materials traceability, customer reputation, and destructive or sample testing for safety-critical components.
failureModes: Only a limited subset of goods are suitable for local fabrication or repair without industrial tooling.
Warranty, liability, certification, and materials quality requirements may keep many products in centralized supply chains.
Local cells may be less cost efficient than high-throughput regional warehouses for standardized goods.
adoptionPath: Begin with returns triage, refurbishment, packaging reuse, spare parts, fixtures, and non-safety-critical custom components.
Use open automation and local materials workflows to improve unit economics before expanding into regulated or high-liability products.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Local production and repair cells directly decentralize parts of inventory handling and fulfillment, but only for product categories that tolerate local manufacturing.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The coordination model is plausible for repair and spare parts, but broad adoption depends on standard design files, trust, quality control, and manufacturer participation.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Open automation exists, but broad microfactory substitution for logistics real estate remains early and constrained by tooling, materials, and certification.

incumbentPressure.rationale: This would pressure selected long-tail logistics flows rather than Prologis' core high-throughput distribution footprint in the near term.

products.name: Prologis Essentials
products.summary: A suite of operational, energy, mobility, and sustainability services packaged for Prologis customers, including warehouse equipment, solar, EV charging, and related site solutions.
products.whyItMatters: Essentials lets Prologis extend from landlord into operating infrastructure, increasing customer dependence on the company's bundled services around power, charging, automation, and warehouse setup.
replacementSketch: The realistic replacement is an interoperable stack of open energy management, open EV charging protocols, transparent equipment procurement, and independent local installers.
Customers would still need physical installation and support, but open standards can make those services portable across landlords and sites.

alternatives.name: OpenEMS
alternatives.summary: Open-source energy management platform for orchestrating renewable generation, storage, grid connection, and loads.

openness.rationale: OpenEMS describes itself as a free and open-source energy management system and publishes its software on GitHub.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It supports distributed energy orchestration across generation, storage, grid, and load assets, fitting localized energy control well.

readiness.rationale: The project presents an active community and production-oriented modular architecture, though commercial deployments still need hardware integration and operations support.

costLeverage.rationale: Open energy management can reduce vendor lock-in for solar, storage, and load-control services and improve bargaining power with installers.

alternatives.name: Open Charge Point Protocol
alternatives.summary: Open protocol for communication between EV charge points and central management systems, designed to support interoperable charging infrastructure.

openness.rationale: OCPP is published by the Open Charge Alliance as a standardized protocol for EV charging communication, with independent implementations available.

decentralizationFit.rationale: A protocol layer helps charger owners avoid single-vendor management systems and supports multi-operator charging networks.

readiness.rationale: OCPP is widely treated as a charging interoperability standard, though real deployments still depend on certification, security, and backend quality.

costLeverage.rationale: Interoperability can reduce lock-in and improve hardware procurement flexibility, but installation, utility interconnection, and demand charges remain major cost drivers.

disruptionConcepts.name: Tenant-owned energy and charging stack
disruptionConcepts.summary: Warehouse tenants could use open energy management and interoperable EV charging protocols to own or competitively source solar, storage, load control, and fleet charging services instead of accepting a landlord-controlled bundle.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept turns Essentials-style bundled infrastructure into a portable, standards-based tenant capability, pressuring Prologis' ability to capture service margin on energy and charging.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization is central through local generation, tenant-controlled energy management, and interoperable charging standards; Bitcoin is not necessary for the mechanism.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Tenants, landlords, installers, chargers, batteries, solar assets, and fleet operators coordinate through open EMS integrations, OCPP-compatible charging management, utility tariffs, and site-level operating rules.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Metered energy flows, charger transaction logs, signed device telemetry, utility bills, and equipment certification constrain false reporting; independent audits can compare generated, stored, consumed, and billed energy.
failureModes: Landlord roof rights, lease terms, and interconnection queues can prevent tenants from controlling energy assets.
Open protocols do not automatically solve cybersecurity, uptime, or fleet charging support obligations.
Utilities and demand charges may make economics unattractive at some sites.
adoptionPath: Start with OCPP-compatible fleet chargers and OpenEMS-managed storage at sites where tenants already control enough electrical infrastructure.
Expand into landlord-tenant contracts that preserve tenant data access, hardware portability, and competitive service procurement.
Aggregate multiple sites into virtual power plant or demand-response programs once telemetry and dispatch controls are trusted.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The model directly moves energy and charging control from a bundled landlord service toward tenant-controlled distributed assets and open protocols.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The technical coordination primitives exist, but site access, utility interconnection, and lease rights remain hard constraints.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Open EMS and OCPP-compatible charging are deployable today, though industrial-scale fleet charging requires careful engineering and support.

incumbentPressure.rationale: This could pressure Prologis' energy and mobility service bundle faster than it pressures core rent, because interoperability gives tenants more procurement leverage.
